coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic H2O2 production and removal of Cr (VI) via a novel Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure with broad spectral response

coredata.dc:description: The low-usage of solar energy and the sluggish separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons/holes pairs are the obstacles in the practical application of photocatalysts. The integration of upconversion and Z-scheme heterojunction is expected to break the barriers to achieve the efficient charge separation and broaden near-infrared light absorption. Herein, an effective indirect Z scheme AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure with carbon quantum dots (CQDs, as the electron conduction medium) and Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho (as upconversion function) has been successfully synthesized. Consequently, the Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure exhibited superior photocatalytic activities for Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 production, reducing 99.9% of Cr(VI)(20 ppm, 15 min) and 78.5% of Cr(VI) (40 ppm, 30 min) with visible light irradiation as well as 94.0% of Cr(VI) (20 ppm, 39 min) under NIR light irradiation. Simultaneously, the heterostructure could generate 902.9 μM H2O2 for 5 h under visible light irradiation. The intensive photocatalytic properties could primarily be attributed to the boosted light absorption capacity, the improved solar-to-energy conversion by the remarkable upconversion capacity of Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs and the faster charge transfer through a Z-schematic pathway. This work is anticipated to open a novel “window” for designing the efficient photocatalysts by coupling of Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho and CQDs.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic H2O2 production and removal of Cr (VI) via a novel Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure with broad spectral response

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The low-usage of solar energy and the sluggish separation efficiency of the photogenerated electrons/holes pairs are the obstacles in the practical application of photocatalysts. The integration of upconversion and Z-scheme heterojunction is expected to break the barriers to achieve the efficient charge separation and broaden near-infrared light absorption. Herein, an effective indirect Z scheme AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure with carbon quantum dots (CQDs, as the electron conduction medium) and Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho (as upconversion function) has been successfully synthesized. Consequently, the Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure exhibited superior photocatalytic activities for Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 production, reducing 99.9% of Cr(VI)(20 ppm, 15 min) and 78.5% of Cr(VI) (40 ppm, 30 min) with visible light irradiation as well as 94.0% of Cr(VI) (20 ppm, 39 min) under NIR light irradiation. Simultaneously, the heterostructure could generate 902.9 μM H2O2 for 5 h under visible light irradiation. The intensive photocatalytic properties could primarily be attributed to the boosted light absorption capacity, the improved solar-to-energy conversion by the remarkable upconversion capacity of Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs and the faster charge transfer through a Z-schematic pathway. This work is anticipated to open a novel “window” for designing the efficient photocatalysts by coupling of Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho and CQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: As a promising technology, photocatalysis in solar energy conversion has attracting an amount of interest in the current circumstances of increased energy demand and environmental pollution problem (Liu et al., 2021a; Zhang et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2020; Gao et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2019; Dang et al., 2021). Unfortunately, owing to the exhibition of the low-usage in solar energy or considerable poor stability upon photoirradiation as well as low separation of charge carriers, the photocatalytic systems still have not been scaled up to industrial applications until now (Liu et al., 2015). Thus, it has considerable high aspiration to design the wide-spectral responsive and high quantum efficiency semiconductor-based photocatalysts.

As intriguing visible-light-response photocatalysts, metallic sulfides (e.g., In2S3, AgInS2, ZnIn2S4 and CdIn2S4) are attracting large-scale attention because of the tunable optical properties and unique electronic structures as well as the ideal band gaps (Zhou et al., 2021a,b; Du et al., 2019a,b; Qiu et al., 2021; Li et al., 2020), offering a favorable platform for photocatalytic reaction. Nevertheless, the performance upon single metallic sulfides photocatalysts is still not considerable owning the sluggish electrons/holes (e- / h+) pairs separation. Thus, searching more sustainable methods will be indispensable for enhancing efficiently the electrons/holes pairs separation. A considerable feasible methodology is the construction of heterogeneous (Iwase et al., 2016; Qiu et al., 2019), such as type-II heterostructures and direct Z-Scheme systems (Khan et al., 2020; Di et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2019), especially looking forward to boost the catalytic activity owning its own advantages (Chen et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2018), such as the shorter diffusion lengths of charge carriers, greater specific surface area and much richer active sites (Iwashina et al., 2015). The superior performance shown upon heterojunction is mainly attributed to the accelerated charge separation and the greater long-term stability, which are caused by the built-in potential field formed in nanosized interface. In addition, e- and h+ generated upon type-II heterostructures exhibit much weaker redox ability on account of the circular movement (Liang et al., 2021a). Moreover, another issue that needs to be addressed is about harvesting broad spectrum light from 46% visible light to 49% NIR light (Tian et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2017; Gao et al., 2019). NIR light cannot directly motivate most semiconductors in view of band gap width. Thus, opening a “window” for utilizing NIR light is still challenging.

As the powerful assistant, converting low-energy excited light into high-energy emitted light through a multiphoton absorption process, upconversion materials have been applied to bedeck semiconductors for harvesting broad spectrum light to NIR light. For instance, the reported NaYF4:Yb3+,Tm3+@NaYF4:Yb3+, Nd3+@TiO2 core@shell nanoparticles by Huang et al. induced superior photocatalytic performance, which was attributable to the properties of the upconversion material itself (Huang et al., 2019). Although upconversion materials show excellent performance in broadening light absorption scope to NIR light, the absorption capacity of light is still weakness in the short-wavelength light region. A large of tremendous attentions have been attracted upon carbon quantum dots (CQDs, an emerging class of carbon material family) due to its own unique microstructure and optical properties, accounting for the improved adsorption capacity and strong optical absorption in CQDs-based photocatalytic systems (Xie et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021a,b; Martindale et al., 2015). As the electron mediator, CQDs have also made great achievements in boosting the photogenerated electrons transfer in the Z-scheme heterojunction system (Feng et al., 2020; Liang et al., 2020). Over Z-Scheme systems (Xu et al., 2018), under the action of electrostatic attraction, the e- can divert convincingly from conduction band position (CB) of one semiconductor to the valence band (VB) of other semiconductor with enriching h+, which is no similar migration path of charge carriers with type II heterostructures system. Apparently, to build direct Z-scheme CQDs-based heterostructure systems could show unambiguously much better redox catalytic performance.

Herein, based mainly on above particular considerations, the Z-scheme heterostructured Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 nanocomposite with MIL-68(In) as a template was designed and synthesized, exhibiting superior photocatalytic activities in Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 production. The overall synthetic strategy involved various preparation methods, such as high temperature solid phase, hydrothermal and ion exchange reaction methods. The results showed that the energy transfer processes existed in Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure according to room temperature UC emission spectra, and the coupling of CQDs and Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho could enhance the light absorption capacity for the enhanced usage of solar energy. The formation of the indirect Z-scheme between CQDs and AgInS2/In2S3 was determined by band structures and PL spectra test. Coupling of Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho and CQDs with AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure can provide the shorter diffusion lengths of charge carriers to accelerate charge transfer and improve the ability to the optical absorption, which can be largely contributive to the excellent photocatalytic H2O2 production and removal of Cr (VI). We believe that the new ideas are put forward on synthesizing multifunction Z-scheme heterojunctions for environmental treatment and H2O2 production in this work.

All the chemicals involving in synthetic catalysts were of analytical reagent grade and no further purification. Niobium oxide (purity 99.9%), lutetium oxide (purity 99.99%), ytterbium oxide (purity 99.99%), holmium oxide (purity 99.99%), terephthalic acid (purity 99%) and indium (Ⅲ) nitrate tetrahydrate (purity 99.9%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemistry Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium citrate (purity 99%) and silver nitrate (purity 99.8%) were from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and while thiourea (purity 99%) was from Aladdin Industrial Corporation.

Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho upconversion material was synthesized by high temperature solid phase method according to the previous strategy (Liao et al., 2019). In detail, 5.34 mmol Lu2O3, 2 mmol Nb2O5, 0.6 mmol Yb2O3 and 0.06 mmol Ho2O3 were putted in an agate mortar, and a small amount of absolute ethanol was added and grinded for half an hour until the ethanol was completely volatilized. And then it was transferred to the muffle furnace, being calcined at 1400 ℃ in the heating rate of 5 ℃/min for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho upconversion material can be obtained, and labeled as LYH.

The LYH/CQDs was obtained on the basis of the related literature with modification (Li et al., 2017). 5.34 mmol citric acid was under dissolution in 60 mL deionized water so that obtaining a uniform solution. 2.01 mL ethylenediamine solution and LYH suspension (0.1 g LYH was under dissolution in 6 mL deionized water) were added dropwise under constant stirring. After 30 min, it was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon autoclave as well as heated at 200 ℃ for 5 h. After cooled to room temperature, the obtained product was centrifuged and washed with ethanol, and then dried at 80 ℃ for 6 h.

MIL-68(In) metal organic framework was prepared with a little of modification according to the report (Jing et al., 2017). In(NO3)3 and H2BDC with 1:2 mol ratio were dissolved in 100 mL of DMF solution for stirring for 5 min, and then heated at 120 ℃ in an oil bath for 3 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, it was pumped and washed three times with anhydrous ethanol in order to eliminate redundant DMF stuck in the pores. The ivory MIL-68(In) can be obtained and dried at 60 ℃ for 6 h under vacuum. Afterwards, 1 g MIL-68(In) was added into thiourea ethanol solution (2 g thiourea being dissolved in 40 mL ethanol) for stirring for 1 h. The mixed solution was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL in capacity) and it was holded at 200 ℃ for 5 h. The yellow precipitate can be gathered by centrifugation and washing with ethanol and deionized water for three times after cooling to room temperature, and then dried at 60 ℃ for 10 h and named as IS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of AgInS2 and AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: AgInS2 and AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunction were obtained via an efficient cation exchange method over In2S3 (Wang et al., 2017). Specifically, 0.3 g IS was dispersed fully in 40 mL deionized water, afterwards a certain amount of AgNO3 was added under constant stirring, respectively. As the amount of AgNO3 increased, the solution gradually changed from the original yellow to dark yellow. After stirring for 2 h, it was centrifuged, washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water for three times, and next dried at 60 ℃ for 10 h. AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunctions with different mass ratios could be obtained, and marked as AIS-1/IS, AIS-2/IS as well as AIS-3/IS, respectively. For the preparation of AgInS2, the synthesis method was consistent with the preparation of AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunction, except that the excessive AgNO3 was added to the IS suspension, and named as AIS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunction
ce:section.ce:para: 0.3 g IS and 0.067 g AgNO3 were dispersed or dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water, respectively. After stirring for 0.5 h, AgNO3 solution was dropped into the IS solution. After stirring again for 2 h, 0.1 g Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho upconversion material was added and stirred for 0.5 h. Finally, the sample was collected by centrifuged, washed alternately with absolute ethanol and deionized water for three times, and dried at 60 ℃ for 10 h. It was named as LYH/AIS-3/IS. For Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunction, the preparation process was consistent with the above process. The alternative was 0.1 g of Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs, the sample was named as LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS.

Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer was operated for testing the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of samples with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The morphology and structure of obtained samples were characterized through scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (QUANTA F250) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) (JEM-2100 F). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) measurements were conducted on PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system, equipping with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source to gain surface chemical composition, and all binding energies were referenced to the characteristic of C1s line at 284.8 eV. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm was obtained to test the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas as well as porous structures by means of automatic analyzer (ASAP2020). The Fourier transformed infrared spectra (FTIR) were in measurements on a Nicolet 5700 FT-IR spectrometer by KBr pellets. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu), in which BaSO4 powder was took as the internal reflectance sample to collect the optical properties over a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The upconversion (UC) luminescence spectra were performed by Edinburgh Instruments Model FLS980 spectrometer, coupling with NIR laser (λ = 980 nm) as the excitation source. Furthermore, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were tested in a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4500, Japan) with the excitation wavelength of 300 nm. The electrochemical measurements were detected in an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, China) coupled with a traditional three electrode cell, in which Ag/AgCl electrode was performed as the reference electrode and platinum wires was as the counter electrode. The working electrode was fabricated on fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass, which was cleaned by ethanol and deionized water firstly. The slurry was conducted on 10 mg samples being in dispersion in 1 mL ethanol by ultrasound, afterwards dip-coated on the surface of the FTO glass with the area of 1 cm2. The samples were further dried at 50 °C for 2 h under nature condition to enhance the adhesion.

The photocatalytic H2O2 production was conducted on 300W Xenon lamp with light intensity of 120 mW cm−2, which consisted of visible and NIR light with 420 nm cut-off filter. 20 mg sample was in dissolution with 18 mL deionized water, then 2 mL absolute ethanol was added, transferred to the high-pressure reactor. Before irradiation, oxygen was added into the reactor to exhaust the air. After 10 min, the pneumatic valve was closed and it kept at 0.2 Pa pressure of oxygen atmospheres under 20 °C. During the illumination process, 1 mL solution was gathered by syringe at certain intervals and filtered by 0.45 µm filter membrane, and the yield of H2O2 could be tested by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer. In detail, 500 μL of supernatant could react with 2 mL of KI solution (0.1 M) and 50 μL of ammonium paramolybdate (H24Mo7N6O24.4H2O, 0.01 M). The absorbance at λ = 352 nm was determined by ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer after being dispersed ultrasonically for 10 min.

The photocatalytic activity of photoreduction of chromium (VI) (20 mg/L, 40 mg/L) was evaluated in visible or NIR irradiation. For the visible light photocatalytic experiments, it was operated under 400 W metal halide lamp (the light intensity of 36 mW cm−2) with a UV filter of less than 420 nm and another more than 760 nm IR filter, and it was carried out with the NIR photocatalytic experiments under the 980 nm diode laser, equipping with a beam expander (VA-I -DC-980, VIASHO). In a representative photocatalytic evaluation experiment, 30 mg photocatalyst was under dissolution in 50 mL Cr(VI) solution, and it kept under room temperature by injecting the circulated cooled water into the reactor throughout the whole experiment. Before the irradiation, the adsorption-desorption equilibrium should be accomplished by through magnetic stir for 40 min in the dark. During the illumination reaction process, 3–4 mL of liquid supernatant at certain interval by being centrifuged completely to remove the catalyst was collected. The Cr (VI) concentration was monitored with the help of 1, 5-diphenylcarbazide colorimetric method by measuring the supernatant in UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-6300 Double Beam Spectrophotometer). Specifically, 1 mL of supernatant was injected into volumetric flask (10 mL) with 9 mL of 0.2 M H2SO4 solution. After that, 300 μL 1,5-diphenylcarbazide solution was blended and shaken uniformly with the above mixed solution. A few minutes later, a purple solution would form, which was detected the absorption strength at 540 nm by UV–vis spectrophotometer. The reduction efficiency (η) could be obtained according to the following expression:

The overall synthetic strategy involves various preparation methods as shown in Fig. 1a. As the precursor, the uniform In-based MOF (MIL-68) rod-like shape with a high aspect ratio was prepared successfully through solvothermal process of In3+ ions with 1, 4 benzenedicarboxylic acid organic ligand (Fig. 1b) (Pi et al., 2019). IS sample presents the assembled finer nanorods after reacting with thiourea via hydrothermal method (Fig. 1c). Afterward, through a cation exchange reaction with Ag+, IS sample can be transformed into the thicker rod-like AIS (Fig. 1d). Interestingly, coupling of LYH with AIS-3/IS heterojunction can control the transformation of In2S3 in a thicker direction through cation exchange reaction, LYH/AIS-3/IS sample preserves rod-like morphology with about 200 nm in diameter from the local enlarged view in Fig. 1e and f, and tiny nanoparticles may belong to the LYH. No noticeable change in morphology can be detected on the surface of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample after adding CQDs (Figs. 1g and S1).

To further reveal the information of microscopic morphology as well as phase structure, the TEM and HRTEM of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample were performed in Fig. 2(a–d) and Fig. S2. The TEM image confirms the existence of the rod-like shape on LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample (Fig. 1c and 2a), and the magnified side view of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample demonstrates the numerous nanoparticles distributed on the surface in Fig. 2b. Abundant of CQDs are evenly attached to the surface of AIS-3/IS (Fig. 2c), which can explain the successful formation of CQDs/AIS-3/IS material. Specifically, CQDs was successfully synthesized with the averaged size of 4–5 nm, and the lattice distance of CQDs was measured as 0.21 nm, matched with the (100) crystal plane (Hua et al., 2018). The magnified side view also reveals the successful attraction of LYH on the surface of AIS-3/IS (Figs. 2d and S2), the lattice distance of d = 0.310 nm belongs to the (111) crystal plane of cubic Lu3NbO7. Fig. 2e shows the elemental mappings of individual LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS material, indicating that C, O, In, Ag, S, Lu, Nb, Yb and Ho elements were uniformly distributed over the whole nanostructure. And the EDX spectrum of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample also can confirm the existence of every element (Fig. S3 and Table S1) and the amount of elements is proximity to the theoretical design of photocatalyst preparation. All above further reveal the successful formation of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS material.

The phase structures and crystallinities of as-prepared samples can be identified by XRD patterns and shown in Fig. 3. Particularly, IS and AIS samples keep a similar crystalline structure and there appear no other diffraction peaks, such as corresponding to Ag2S (Lei et al., 2020). Obviously, the diffraction peaks of AIS sample only show an obvious difference in intensity based on the comparison with IS sample, demonstrating the addition of Ag+ can affect the crystallinity of IS to a large extent. The peak at 2θ = 12.39° corresponds to the crystal plane of (111) for In2S3. These peaks at 2θ = 17.52°, 18.30° and 23.90° could be assigned to the crystal planes of (311), (222) and (220) for AgInS2. LYH presents the strong peaks at 2θ = 29.82°, 34.55°, 49.70°, and 59.05°, corresponding to the crystal planes of (111), (200), (220), and (311) for cubic Lu3NbO7 (JCPDS No.24–1089) (Liao et al., 2019; İlhan1 et al., 2017), respectively. More importantly, LYH/AIS-3/IS and LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS samples present the same strong peaks belonging to cubic Lu3NbO7, showing LYH material was successfully in integration with AIS-3/IS catalyst. The diffraction peaks are consistent with each other and there are in presence of high crystallinities in materials. And no evidence can be found that the peaks can be matched with MIL-68(In) samples, suggesting no presence of MIL-68(In) in other obtained samples such as IS, AIS, AIS-3/IS and so on. Moreover, no other impurity phase can be detected, suggesting the crystalline structure was well maintained in all samples obtained by ion exchange and precipitation. In addition, since the content of CQDs is too low to reach the detection limit of XRD, the diffraction peaks of CQDs could not be detected.

Revealing the chemical structures of all elements on the surfaces of AIS, AIS-3/IS and LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS samples, X-ray photoelectron spectra were performed in Fig. 4. For LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS (Fig. 4a), the characteristic of the Ag+ state is corresponding with two binding energies centered at 367.97 and 373.95 eV of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2 (Du et al., 2019b), respectively. In 3d5/2 and In 3d3/2 orbitals appear two distinct peaks at 445.07 and 452.60 eV(Fig. 4b). The two peaks located at 161.43 and 162.63 eV belong to the S2- on the composite (Fig. 4c), ascribed to S 2P3/2 and S 2P1/2 (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017; Lei et al., 2020; Du et al., 2019). On AIS-3/IS, it can be observed dazzlingly a clear shift of Ag and S elements with comparison to pure AIS sample, meaning the electron transfer between AIS and IS. Even more, it can also be observed evidently that LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample shifts ~0.2 eV to higher binding energy than AIS-3/IS sample, unraveling the strong electron coupling effect of CQDs and LYH in the photocatalyst system (Qiu et al., 2020). In comparison with AIS and AIS-3/IS, the peaks of Ag 3d, In 3d, and S 2p over LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS present an obvious slight shift, suggesting the presence of the strong chemical interactions with each other on the composite and an change of chemical environment. The deconvoluted C 1s XPS spectra are displayed in Fig. 4d, one peak positioned at 284.8 eV is highly corresponding to the sp2 orbit C-C bond, and the other one at 286.35 eV is matched with the C-O-C bond (Li et al., 2010; Cao et al., 2011; Martindale et al., 2015; Li et al., 2017). The binding energies at 206.70, 199.40, and 197.40 eV are belonging to the Lu 4d, Yb 4d and Nb 3d, respectively, and the another two peaks positioned at 161.37 and 162.53 eV are corresponding to Ho4d (Fig. 4e-g) for LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample. Therefore, it can confirm the presence of CQDs and the strong interaction with each other through XPS results.

The pore-size distributions and specific surface areas were analyzed in virtue of N2 adsorption/desorption measurement (Fig. S4a), and the relevant parameters of as-synthesized catalysts were systematically summarized as listed in Table S2. The IV type adsorption-desorption isotherms indicated the presence of mesoporous structures in as-synthesized samples. And no noticable change can been seen in specific surface area, indicting it is not the sole determinant to enhance the photocatalytic activity. In addition, the corresponding BJH pore-size distribution curves can be attributed to H1 isotherms with the lag ring (Fig. S4b), suggesting the existence of accumulation holes. At the same time, the pore structures in the texture are almost consistent and they have a wide pore size distribution at 20–30 nm.

Furthermore, as showed in Fig. S5, compared with the pure samples of AgInS2 and In2S3, the composite of upconversion material and carbon dots has no significant influence on the main structure of AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunction. The prepared sample has a strong absorption peak at 1640 cm−1, which belongs to the H-O-H bending vibration peak of water molecules. 3445 cm−1 and 3845 cm−1 belong to the characteristic stretching vibration peak of hydroxyl groups, in which water molecules may be attached to the catalyst surface (Li et al., 2019; Farmer 1974). Two kinds of stretching vibration peaks at 1000–1200 and 608 cm−1 correspond to the In-S bond (Yuan et al., 2019). The peak of near 1514.5 cm−1 may be caused by Ag-S stretching vibration (Mohadesi., 2016), and with the increase of AgInS2 content, the peak increases gradually. The peak at 3700 cm−1 reinforces with the increase of AgInS2, and reaches the maximum on AIS-3/IS, indicating that AgInS2 and In2S3 form a heterojunction. On samples of LYH/AIS-3/IS and LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS, the peak at 648 cm−1 corresponds to Nb-O bond, and the introduction of LYH material weakens the peak at 648 cm−1, which proves the strong interaction between LYH and AgInS2/In2S3 heterojunction [Mcdevitt et al., 1964].

The optical absorption properties are of great importance for photocatalytic applications, UV−vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed to evaluate the optical absorption performances as displayed in Fig. 5. The absorption edges of AIS and IS samples are about 800 nm and 650 nm, respectively, and while the range of spectral response is extended largely to visible light region. An noticable red-shift in the absorption threshold can be found in the Z-scheme heterojunction, causing an change in the band gap. And integrating LYH and CQDs could slightly strengthen the absorption to visible light. All band gap energies were calculated and showed in Table S3. The constructing AIS/IS heterojunction is effective to adjust the band gap, which is conducive to broaden the optical absorption. Such findings confirm that as-synthesized samples could be enough induced by visible light irradiation to produce electrons/holes pairs for photocatalytic H2O2 production and removal of Cr (VI).

To further reveal the reason about the increased optical absorption upon LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS, the optical properties of LYH and LYH/CQDs materials in Fig. 6a were characterized. LYH/CQDs presents an apparent variety in color, changing from white of bare LYH to pale yellow of LYH/CQDs, showing the successful addition of CQDs on LYH material. Moreover, a distinct broad peak located at 364 nm is corresponding exactly to the CO (4.1 eV, 300 nm, n → π*) and CN (2.9 eV, 421 nm, n→ π*) (Li et al., 2017). This makes clear that CQDs are responsible for enhancing light absorptive capacity and CQDs can be successfully synthesized, which is consistent with XPS analysis.

Moreover, the upconversion properties of as-synthesized samples were measured with 980 nm laser excitation. As presented in Fig. 6b, LYH, LYH/AIS-3/IS, and LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS samples show two typical emission peaks at 511–577 nm and 632–708 nm with different intensities, assigning to the transition of (5F4,5S2)→5I8 and 5F5→5I8 (Liao et al., 2019; Reddy et al., 2017) ( Fig. 7a) and leading to the output different colors light (green emission and red emission), respectively. The results reveal that the luminescent intensities of LYH/AIS-3/IS and LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS present an obvious decrease compared to that of naked LYH, indicating the energy can be transferred from LYH to AIS-3/IS semiconductor. The CIE coordinates were calculated and displayed in Fig. 7b, and the coordinates of{a (0.4409, 0.5497), b (0.4568, 0.5291) and c (0.4959, 0.4875)} are corresponding to Lu3NbO7:Yb,Ho, LYH/AIS-3/IS and LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS, respectively.

On the other hand, it can also strongly estimate the electrons/holes separation/recombination rate by room temperature PL characterization (Liao et al., 2017). The results indicate that a remarkable fluorescence quenching exists over AIS/IS sample compared with naked IS material (Fig. 6c), and the PL intensity of AIS-3/IS sample is lowest, indicating the repressed recombination of photogenerated carriers in the heterostructure. It is largely contributive to the excellent photoactivity. Interestingly, the PL intensity can be boosted after integrating with LYH material over AIS-3/IS sample, and it can be further enhanced after coupling of CQDs, which may be ascribed that LYH material itself generates fluorescence and the addition of CQDs as a medium can mediate electrons on the AgInS2 transfer and recombinate with holes on the In2S3, meaning a Z-scheme electron transfer.

The separation rates of photogenerated carriers of as-synthesized samples were also estimated over photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). The greater photocurrent and lower impedance mean better light response capability and higher separation efficiency of e-/h+ pairs. It can strongly illustrate the presence of the facilitated transfer kinetics of photoinduced carriers over as-synthesized samples, which generates the boosted photocurrent densities ( Fig. 8a). The same result can also be seen in presence of Cr(VI) species (Fig. S6). The photocurrent density of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample is still higher than that of IS, but lower than that in the Na2SO4 solution, suggesting that the generated electrons on the LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS can be in combination with Cr(VI) and then reduced to Cr(Ⅲ). In addition, it indicated that LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS can provide much more electrons to participate into the reaction. Moreover, the semicircle in the high-frequency range is in line with the charge transfer resistance. Z-scheme heterostructure exhibits the most depressed semicircles at high frequency (Fig. 8b), indicating the smaller resistance and faster charge transfer kinetics. The photoelectrochemical results suggest that LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS has the optimized efficiency of charge carriers separation, which is attributed potentially to the unique heterojunction structure and the enhanced range of optical response.

Evaluations of the reducibilities of samples were studied in three electrode system. The modified samples as working electrode were directly operated in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) via linear scan voltammetry (LSV). For comparison, the performances over naked IS and AIS samples were carried out in the uniform circumstance. As illustrated in Fig. 9a, the negligible HER activities are shown on bare IS and AIS samples. As expected, LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS electrode presents the better HER activity and very lower overpotential among these modified samples. These results suggest that the integrations of LYH and CQDs with AIS/IS heterojunction are beneficial for improving the catalytic reducibilities. Furthermore, Tafel plots were applied to evaluate the reducibility reaction kinetics (Fig. 9b). Generally, the ideal and efficient catalytic materials have low Tafel slope. Obviously, the slope for LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample is 0.68 V dec−1, which is lower than that of other materials, further suggesting the superior HER kinetics and the better catalytic reducibilities.

Furthermore, to further reveal the reason of better reducibilities over LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS, the electrode was also used to measure the electrochemical specific active area (ECSA), proportional to the double-layer capacitances (Cdl) (McCrory et al., 2013). In the non-faradaic potential range, the Cdl could be investigated via cyclic voltammetry (CV) with different scan rates, and they can be determined by fitting the capacitive currents via the scan rates. As provided in Fig. 10, the Cdl value of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample was calculated to be 13.45 μF cm−2, which is better than that of other materials. The higher ECSA can promote the higher catalytic activities. It indicates that integrating LYH and CQDs with AIS/IS heterojunction can not only optimize the charge transfer kinetics, but also magnify the surface active area.

The visible light irradiation of all samples can drive the O2 reduction to H2O2 in ethanol aqueous solution (10 vol%), and it shows the result of time-dependent H2O2 concentration over all samples ( Fig. 11). It can be seen that AIS shows very less photocatalytic activity, suggesting its inferiority for H2O2 production. Obviously, AIS/IS heterojunction exhibits the enhanced photocatalytic activity in generating H2O2 based on the comparison with AIS and IS samples, which can be in consequence of the excellent structure that adjusts the separation of electrons and holes. The further incorporation of LYH and CQDs into AIS/IS heterojunction can significantly improve the amount of H2O2 production (902.9 μM H2O2 for 5 h), which is ascribed to the boosted light absorption of visible light and fast electron transfer. These results suggest that the excellent structure and the boosted light absorption can increase together the yield of H2O2, which is produced from the revivification of O2. The trapping experiments of active species on H2O2 production was performed over LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample. And the result is showed in Fig. S7, the activity of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample was at a disadvantage for H2O2 production under N2 environment, and it still locates at inferior position after adding benzoquinone (BQ), suggesting the essential role of O2 and •O2 − for H2O2 production. Beside, most g-C3N4-based photocatalysts used in the literature ( Table 1) exhibited lower activity for H2O2 production than that of the prepared LYH/CQDs/AIS/IS heterojunction.

The feasibilities of reducing different concentrations of Cr(VI) over as-synthesized samples were initially evaluated under visible as well as NIR light irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 12, all synthesized samples show the different photocatalytic performances under the different light sources, indicating that light source is of great importance in enhanced photocatalytic reactions. In detail, IS and AIS samples show poor activities on Cr(VI) reduction, which may because that the generated electrons and holes cannot separate without delay. However, AIS/IS heterojunction can strongly accelerate the Cr(VI) reduction with the comparison of naked IS and AIS samples, which may be ascribed to the heterojunctions that optimize electron transfer paths, in accordance with previous reported literatures (McCrory et al., 2013). It's worth distinctly noting that LYH is introduced into AIS-3/IS sample (Fig. 12a and b), the removal efficiency of 20 ppm Cr(VI) reduction can be enhanced from 76.3% achieved by AIS-3/IS to 98.0% over LYH/AIS-3/IS composite under visible light illumination for 15 min. And with the decoration of CQDs, the performance of Cr(VI) reduction can further be increased, which is attributed to the further absorption and utilization of light due to CQDs. Moreover, LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS composite also shows the superior performance for 40 ppm Cr(VI) reduction, reducing 78.5% of Cr(VI) but 24.1% and 14.9% for IS and AIS samples under visible light illumination for 30 min, respectively (Fig. 12c and d). In addition, LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS composite could also make a bigger contribution on 20 ppm Cr(VI) reduction under NIR light illumination, reducing 94.0% Cr(VI) for 39 min, and while there are only 43.5% and 12.5% for IS and AIS samples (Fig. 12e and f). XPS analysis can further confirm the valence state of Cr over LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample (Fig. S8). Three components could be curve-fitted at 576.2 eV, 577.5 eV and 586.8 eV of the high resolution spectra of Cr 2p peaks. The first two components belong to Cr 2p3/2 orbitals of the signals of Cr(III), and the last one can be attributed to Cr 2p1/2 orbitals, corresponding to Cr(VI) (Wu et al., 2018). The Cr(VI) existence may be owning to the adsorption on LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample surface. Therefore, XPS analysis result suggest that the Cr(VI) reduction can be done over LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample. It takes longer times for Cr(VI) reduction over sulfide-based catalysts in the literature ( Table 2), whereas LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS provides a completely superior platform.

Moreover, in order to measure the stability over the best composite photocatalyst of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS, the recycle experiment was carried out for Cr(VI) reduction. As proved in Fig. 13, the overall view of Cr(VI) reduction behavior inspires to the result that as-synthesized sample exits a good performance and high stability for Cr(VI) reduction after maintaining five successive cycles, indicating a promising application in Cr(VI) reduction.

As has been reported that the active species of e- and •O2 − participated in photocatalytic Cr(VI) reduction. To go deep into understanding for the significance of active species and explore the underlying raised photocatalytic performance mechanism, the trapped tests with various radical scavengers were executed upon LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample for 20 ppm of Cr(VI) reduction. In detail, disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (Na2-EDTA, 1 mM), tertbutyl alcohol (t-BuOH, 1 mM) and KBrO3 (1 mM) were participated in capturing photogenerated holes, hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and electrons, respectively. From Fig. 14, it clearly reveals that adding Na2-EDTA or t-BuOH presents slightly negative effect on the Cr(VI) reduction, and while KBrO3 can significantly inhibit the generation of Cr(VI) reduction, denoting that e- as the vital active radical species takes part in the whole Cr(VI) reduction process.

The application of nitrobluetetrazolium (NBT) (a kind of molecular probes) can monitor the production of •O2 − (Majhi et al., 2021, 2020). A new molecular formazan derivative can be produced by combined NBT with O2•‾, which can be detected by UV-Vis absorbance spectra. The decreased absorption intensity with the increasing reaction time suggest the NBT molecule is consumed by O2•‾, which can be used as signal in a photocatalytic system for O2•‾ production with high efficiency. As displayed apparently in Fig. 15, an obvious decrease on absorbance existed in NBT aqueous solution after adding LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample under a period of light illumination, suggesting the generation of O2•‾ production over LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS sample, which in favor of H2O2 production.

In addition, we have also applied terephthalic acid (TA) as capture agent. The application of terephthalic acid (TA) can monitor the production of •OH, because 2-hydroxy terephthalic acid can be formed after trapping •OH radical by TA molecule, which can be recognized by PL spectra. Compared with the PL intensities of LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS and IS samples, the apparent difference of the fluorescence intensity is in existence upon LYH/CQDs/AIS-3/IS and pure IS samples. However, this intensity value is much lower than that reported in the literature. This may be due to that when it was exposed to light, the •OH produced by the water itself can induce the change on PL intensity. In addition, for the thermodynamics, the VB of IS and AIS are 1.305 and 1.11 eV, respectively, which much lower than (EOH-/ •OH = 1.99 eV). Therefore, •OH was not the main active species in this system.

Further analysis of valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) value is crucial for comprehending the mechanism of improving photocatalytic performance. Therefore, the values were calculated using the following formula:

As presented in Scheme 1, a possible Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism inspired by the above overall analysis was proposed over Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure, where CQDs could boost the light absorptive capacity, Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho upconversion material could efficiently absorb and utilize long wavelength light and heterostructure can accelerate the transfer of carriers. The synergistic effect of these materials together promoted Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 generation. In detail, CQDs enhance the light absorptive capacity of Lu3NbO7:Yb, Ho upconversion material, and then Yb3+ absorb the energy and transit to Ho3+, leading to the transitions between energy levels from 5F4, 5S2 and 5F5 to 5I8 through energy transfer process, accompanied with the emission of green and red light. The emission light can excite heterojunctions to produce electrons/holes pairs, and the e- generated on the conduction band of AgInS2 can transfer to CQDs and be in combination with h+ stayed in the valence band of In2S3, producing a stronger fluorescence, proved from PL results. The CB value is much more negative than O2/H2O2 potential (−0.68 V vs. NHE) and Cr(VI)/Cr(III) potential (−0.51 V vs NHE), so e- on the conduction band of In2S3 can combine with Cr(VI) for reducing easily into Cr (III) and O2 for generating H2O2.

In summary, Z-scheme Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure was prepared and evaluated the photocatalytic performances under visible or NIR light illumination. And the overall synthetic strategy involved multiple approaches, such as ion exchange reaction, high temperature solid phase and hydrothermal methods. The results revealed that Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho/CQDs/AgInS2/In2S3 heterostructure showed the promoted photocatalytic performances for the Cr(VI) reduction and H2O2 production, which was in consequence of the raised light absorption, proved from DRS measurement, the augmented solar-to-energy conversion efficiency from remarkable upconversion capacity of Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho and CQDs, rapid diffusions of charge carriers and much more abundant catalytically active sites from ECSA test. Furthermore, CQDs could act as a reservoir to achieve electron redistribution. It can be anticipated that it can provide a promising “window” for designing efficient photocatalysts by coupling Lu3NbO7: Yb, Ho and CQDs.
